probe_designs.content: Compute per-race-group recall/TPR on the validation set among patients who actually died (label=1). Gap = max(group recall) - min(group recall). A fair model has gap < 0.05; >0.10 indicates the model misses deaths disproportionately in some race groups. Report each group's TPR and the gap. Anchor AUPRC so fairness isn't bought by globally degrading recall.
possible_sources: Hardt et al. 2016 'Equality of Opportunity' (FATML)
Chen et al. 2023 fairlearn clinical evaluation
Obermeyer et al. 2019 'Dissecting racial bias in an algorithm' (Science)

probe_designs.content: Compute per-group AUPRC (precision-recall, mortality-positive) and report the gap between max and min group AUPRC. Unlike recall gap, this surfaces whether precision-quality of death prediction is uniform across race groups. Gap <0.05 healthy; large gap reveals a model that performs qualitatively better for some ethnicities even if TPR looks matched.
possible_sources: Plebani et al. 2024 subgroup AUPRC fairness
fairlearn.metrics.demographic_parity_difference
Suresh & Guttag 2021 'A framework for understanding sources of harm throughout the ML lifecycle'

probe_designs.content: Plot reliability (calibration) curves of predicted death probability vs observed mortality rate, separately per race group, with Brier score and ECE per group. A healthy model has all 5 curves close to diagonal and group ECE within 0.03 of each other. Divergence indicates race-specific over/under-confidence — clinically dangerous since risk scores drive ICU triage.
possible_sources: Niculescu-Mizil & Caruana 2005 calibration of logistic regression
Pleiss et al. 2017 'On fairness and calibration'
MIMIC benchmark calibration best practices (Johnson et al. 2023)

probe_designs.content: Because 'eth' one-hot features are direct inputs, inspect the logistic regression coefficients on eth columns (and TF-IDF features correlated with eth). A large-magnitude eth coefficient means race is being used as a direct predictor. Healthy: eth coefficients near-zero, model relies on clinical notes; Unhealthy: large eth coefficients indicating the model is shortcutting on protected attribute directly.
possible_sources: Bellamy et al. 2019 fairlearn 'mitigating disparate impact'
Zhang et al. 2018 'Mitigating unwanted biases with adversarial learning'
Mehrabi et al. 2021 'A Survey on Bias and Fairness in ML'

probe_designs.content: Inject 5-10% label noise on training mortality labels and retrain; measure per-group recall shift. Healthy: <3% TPR shift per group. Unhealthy: a single group's recall swings >10%, indicating that group's positive predictions are driven by a few borderline samples — a fragile signal of fairness rather than robust learning.
possible_sources: Northcutt et al. 2021 'Pervasive label errors in ML benchmarks' (PLOS One)
Song et al. 2022 'Learning from noisy labels with deep learning'

probe_designs.content: Sweep decision thresholds from 0.1 to 0.9 per race group; for each group find threshold achieving target TPR=0.7. Healthy model: thresholds needed per group are within 0.05 of each other (groups behave similarly). Unhealthy: a group needs a very different threshold → model's risk-score distribution shifts per race, indicating feature-distribution or coefficient mismatch.
possible_sources: Hardt et al. 2016 equality of opportunity thresholds
fairlearn PostProcessingThreshold (ThresholdOptimizer)

probe_designs.content: Compute per-group recall on both train and validation splits; flag groups with >10pt train-vs-val recall gap (overfitting to that group's training positives). Healthy: per-group train-val recall gap <5pt across all 5 groups. Unhealthy: a group with high train recall but low val recall indicates memorization of that group's positives.
possible_sources: Mitchell et al. 2019 'Model Cards for Model Reporting'
Rajkomar et al. 2018 'Scalable and accurate deep learning for electronic health records' (Nature Digital Medicine)

probe_designs.content: Audit per-group positive-class support: count of (group, label=1) in train and val. Flag groups with <50 death positives in val (statistically unreliable recall estimate). Healthy: each group has >100 death positives; Unhealthy: a group with <30 positives yields noisy per-group recall — fairness gap may be artifact of low support rather than bias.
possible_sources: Mitchell et al. 2019 Model Cards (datasheets)
Gebru et al. 2021 'Datasheets for Datasets'
Chen et al. 2021 subgroup sample-size requirements in fairness audits

probe_designs.content: Audit TF-IDF feature density (non-zero fraction) per race group — a sparse/biased note vocabulary per group could mean the model learns group-specific lexical shortcuts rather than clinical signal. Healthy: feature density within 20% across groups; Unhealthy: one group has much lower density → its notes are shorter/noisier, recall gaps may stem from data-quality not model bias.
possible_sources: Chen et al. 2022 clinical NLP MIMIC subgroup text analysis
Johnson & Khanna 2022 MIMIC-IV NLP benchmarks
Rajkomar et al. 2018

probe_designs.content: Compute Vickers decision-curve net-benefit per race group at threshold probabilities 0.05–0.30 (ICU-mortality clinically-relevant range). Healthy: net-benefit curves overlap across groups; Unhealthy: one group has consistently lower net-benefit → clinical utility of acting on predictions is race-dependent, even if AUPRC looks acceptable.
possible_sources: Vickers & Elkin 2006 'Decision curve analysis' (Med Decis Making)
Vickers et al. 2019 net benefit extensions
Rousson & Gönen 2023 decision-curve for fairness in clinical prediction models
